coredata.dc:title: Improved fluoride removal efficiency using novel defluoridation pencil
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Neha
Kumari, Sonu
Khan, Suphiya
coredata.dc:description: A facile route to a novel defluoridation prototype was developed i.e. fluoride (F−) pencil enclosing 3D network porous hydrogel structure consisted of 2D Graphene oxide (GO) sheets and nanomaterials. Green synthesized nanomaterials as aluminium oxide (Al2O3), silver oxide (Ag2O), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with GO were successfully utilized for developed defluoridation pencil. Synthesized graphene-aluminium-silver-carbon quantum dots (GO-Al2O3-Ag2O-CQDs: GAAC) hydrogel showed high adsorption capacity with characterization through analytical techniques such as field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM), x-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive x-ray analysis (EDX), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The adsorption capacity of GAAC hydrogel was as high as 12.04 mg g-1. Developed hydrogel exhibited removal percentage for 2 and 10 mg L-1 F− as 95.5 and 80 %, respectively. Kinetically, GAAC material for F− adsorption obeyed pseudo-second-order model (R2 = 0.999) as compared to pseudo-first-order model. Langmuir adsorption isotherm (R2 = 0.978) proved more favourable than Freundlich model (R2 = 0.928), which confirmed chemisorption processes for F− adsorption. Finally, mechanism showed that F− ion uptake and release of −OH groups which are bonded to the GAAC composite on the gels is connected.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Chemisorption
Fluoride
Graphene oxide
Hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Initial F− concentration and contact time
Comparison of F− adsorption efficiency for various adsorbents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by DBT BIG-BIRAC Ref No. BIRAC/CCAMP0723/BIG-13/18 and DST major project entitled “Low Cost-Renewable Energy Driven (LC-RED) Water Treatment Solution Centre” Ref No. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/124 . We acknowledged bioinformatics centre for providing computational facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram illustrate the synthesis process of Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs, GO, CQDs and 3-D hydrogel.

Chemical structure of the main nanocomposites (a) Aluminium oxide NPs, Silver oxide NPs, Graphene oxide and CQDs, (b) GAAC Hydrogel, (c) SEM images of GAAC hydrogel before adsorption, (d) F− defluoridation pencil and (e) SEM images of GAAC hydrogel after adsorption.
(a) and (b) show FESEM and EDX of Al NPs, (c) and (d) show FESEM and EDX of Ag NPs, (e) and (f) shows FESEM and EDX of GO.
(a) and (b) shows the TEM and EDX of CQDs.
(a) and (b) shows FESEM and EDX of GAAC hydrogel before adsorption, (c) and (d) shows FESEM and EDX of GAAC hydrogel after adsorption.
XRD pattern of (a) Ag2O NPs, (b) Al2O3 NPs and (c) CQDs.
FTIR of GO, Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs, CQDs and GAAC before and after F− adsorption.
Effect of (a) pH (2 mg L−1 F− concentration), (b) Initial F− concentration (2 mg L−1 F− concentration, pH 2, contact time 8 min) and (c) contact time (2 mg L−1 F− concentration, pH 2).
Adsorption isotherms (a) Langmuir, (b) Freundlich and (c) Temkin.
Novel designed of Fluoride removal Pencil having GAAC nanocomposite.
Schematic diagram illustrate mechanism of F− adsorption.
Isotherm models and their linear forms.

row.entry: log qe = log kF + (1/n) log Ce

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Kinetics for F adsorption onto GAAC.
Values of the Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms constants.
Efficiency of defluoridation pencil on the F− removal for drinking water.
Adsorption capacity of present adsorbent with other adsorbents.

row.entry: Magnetic Prussian blue/graphene oxide caged in calcium alginate
Porous three-dimensional graphene foam/Prussian blue composite

head.ce:title: Improved fluoride removal efficiency using novel defluoridation pencil

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, Banasthali Vidyapith, Banasthali Tonk, Rajasthan, 304022, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, Banasthali Vidyapith, Banasthali Tonk, Rajasthan (India) 304022

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology Banasthali University, P.O. Banasthali Vidyapith, Rajasthan, 304022, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology Banasthali University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile route to a novel defluoridation prototype was developed i.e. fluoride (F−) pencil enclosing 3D network porous hydrogel structure consisted of 2D Graphene oxide (GO) sheets and nanomaterials. Green synthesized nanomaterials as aluminium oxide (Al2O3), silver oxide (Ag2O), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with GO were successfully utilized for developed defluoridation pencil. Synthesized graphene-aluminium-silver-carbon quantum dots (GO-Al2O3-Ag2O-CQDs: GAAC) hydrogel showed high adsorption capacity with characterization through analytical techniques such as field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM), x-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive x-ray analysis (EDX), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The adsorption capacity of GAAC hydrogel was as high as 12.04 mg g-1. Developed hydrogel exhibited removal percentage for 2 and 10 mg L-1 F− as 95.5 and 80 %, respectively. Kinetically, GAAC material for F− adsorption obeyed pseudo-second-order model (R2 = 0.999) as compared to pseudo-first-order model. Langmuir adsorption isotherm (R2 = 0.978) proved more favourable than Freundlich model (R2 = 0.928), which confirmed chemisorption processes for F− adsorption. Finally, mechanism showed that F− ion uptake and release of −OH groups which are bonded to the GAAC composite on the gels is connected.

ce:section.ce:para: It affects almost 200 million people worldwide and 66 million peoples in India [1]. Fluoride (F−) is considered the major contributor to drinking water contamination, worldwide. It acts as a double edge sword as its concentration up to 1.5 mg L-1 in water is favourable to humans, but excessive intake (above 1.5 mg L-1) causes fluorosis [2]. Several treatment techniques has been developed aiming to reduce F− level in water, i.e. adsorption, ion exchange, precipitation, electrodialysis, reverse osmosis and membrane filtration for which various functional materials were synthesized mainly of carbon, polymers and hybrids [3].

Currently, the paramount complications overlooked by the water purification sector in adsorption are costly nanomaterial adsorbents, aggregation of nanomaterials, leaching of nanomaterials adsorbents and low adsorption efficiency of adsorbents [4]. Therefore, there is a crucial entail to develop nanomaterials for high-performance purification of drinking water. Therefore, green route was introduced for reducing the nanomaterials cost which is much advanced as it is reproducible, simple operated and results in production of much stable nanomaterial [5].

Other limitation that is the covered in the present study is tendency of nanomaterials to aggregate is the crucial flaw of the nanomaterials, which compromises their adsorption efficiency significantly [6]. Due to inherent disadvantages such as low hydraulic conductivity, poor mechanical rigidity and excessive pressure drop, these fine submicron particles and their aggregates can’t put naked into the solution, despite they have excellent adsorption capacity [7]. Porous carriers such as activated carbon, polymeric sorbents, and other porous carriers are used to immobilise nanomaterials in respect to overcome their current disadvantages [8]. For removal of F− from water, the most suitable adsorbents were the ones synthesized via immobilization of porous materials because of their nanoporous structures, sound skeleton strengths, adaptable surface chemistry and crosslinking matrices [9,10]. Noteworthy heed has to be given to the utilization of 3D cross-linked polymers i.e. hydrogels as well as nanomaterials as adsorbents for highly effective adsorption and restraining nanomaterials leaching, which exhibit high activity, specificity and small diffusion transmit. Prevailing porous structure, superior water swallowing capacity, number of various functional groups, increase surface area, makes the backbone of hydrogel [11]. Diverse nanomaterials are employed for adsorption of contaminants because of their high affinity for the contaminants and increase surface-to-volume area [12].

Adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for F−, its simplicity, versatility and effectiveness are the major criteria for selecting a suitable adsorbent. Herein, we have focused on the development of graphene based monohybrids which consist of uniform dispersion of 2D aluminium oxide (Al2O3)-silver oxide (Ag2O)-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the gels. In this milieu, nanostructure polymers proved extremely efficient for F- removal because of their adsorption capacity, high surface-to-volume ratio, cost-effective and selectivity. Aluminum oxide is reported as efficient nanomaterial with well-known high affinity towards F-. Other than this, graphene oxide (GO) as 2D material acquires high surface area, porosity, hydrophilicity and functional groups such as epoxy group, carboxyl etc, can be introduced to developed 3D network of hydrogels [13]. Recent research shows that the advancement of nanomaterials, either in the form of inorganic nano-frameworks, nanogels, nanocomposite hydrogels; is the future of up-and-coming defluoridation expertise [14,15]. CQDs are quasispherical in shape with low down toxicity, good stability and high-quality quantum yield [16], were incorporated into hydrogels. CQDs exhibited the superior electron accepting and electron donating ability, due to its conjugated π structure [17]. Despite, it is economical inappropriate to utilize the silver oxide in its pure form as a due to its high cost. Generally, silver oxide is utilized as composite as absorbents to save costs [18].

Consequently, waste of pencil peel was employed for the construction of all NPs, GO and CQDs via green route to maintain it cost effective and eco-friendly. No previous literature is available regarding the F− removal using synthesized 3D Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs, CQDs, GO based (GAAC) hydrogel. 3D hydrogel nanomaterials were synthesized in the present study: (a) to avoid the leaching of nanomaterials in aqueous solution; (b) to regulate pore size of the nanomaterials via crosslinker, so that the flow of water in and out can be maintained; (c) for easy swallowing. Different physico-chemical methods, such as high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), X-Ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) and Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) were used to characterize the adsorbent. Based on the experimental data, accomplishment of F− adsorption and its working methodology can be demonstrated via consecutive three steps: (a) Green synthesis of Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs and CQDs using pencil peel waste after being sharpen; (b) Synthesis of GAAC hydrogel by Sol-gel method; (c) Kinetics and isotherm studies were done with proposed mechanism.

Analytical grade reagents and chemicals were used without any further purification. Sodium fluoride (NaF), aluminium sulfate (Al2(SO4)3), silver nitrate (AgNO3), acrylamide, bis-acrylamide and ammonium persulphate (APS) purchased from Himedia. All chemical and reagents stock solution was prepared from millipore ultrapure water.

Waste peel of pencil was utilized for the synthesis of Al2O3 and Ag NPs through green route. NPs were synthesized with minor changes in method as reported earlier [19]. Firstly, peel extract was collected after pencil being sharpen and then ground in grinder. After this, 100 mL Millipore water was taken and 20 g of pencil peel powder was mixed in it. It was then heated for 30 min at 80 °C. The synthesized mixture is made to cool and filtered with Whatman’s No.1 filter paper. It was stored at 4 °C for further experiment i.e. synthesis of respective NPs, GO and CQDs required in this research as depicted in Fig. 1 .

For Al2O3 NPs synthesis, Al2(SO4)3 was mixed with pencil peel extract with 1:3 ratios and stirred constantly. The suspension was kept in microwave for 5 min at 540 W, and produced a yellowish-brown precipitate which was further centrifuged. Distilled water and methanol were used to rinse the precipitate obtained. Finally, the obtained precipitate was oven-dried at 100 °C and stored for further experiment.

For Ag2O NPs production, 25 mL of pencil peel extract was mixed with 225 mL of 1 mM AgNO3. The suspension mixture was further kept at room temperature at 120 rpm in a shaker. Centrifugation was performed at 10,000 rpm for the obtained reaction mixture for 30 min. The precipitate which was obtained was then rinsed twice with distilled water. Finally, the precipitate was oven dried at 100 °C and stock up at –4 °C in a sealed container for extra analysis.

For the GO preparation from graphite pencil lead, modified Hummer’s method was used. In brief, 2 g of finely powdered graphite was combined with 35 mL of 98 % H2SO4 and magnetic stirring was done over an ice-bath. After that, 6 g solid KMnO4 was mixed into the mixture and vigorous stirring is done sustaining the temperature under 20 °C to generate graphite solution. The reaction mixture for continuously whipped for 2 h more at room temperature for oxidizing the un-oxidized left-over graphite. The final mixture was diluted via addition of 90 mL of Millipore water and it formed a yellow paste with effervescence. The temperature was about 98 °C. Magnetic stirring of the dilute mixture was done for another 4 h and 30 % H2O2 solution was added to the mixture [13]. Finally, the mixture filtered via Whatman’s No.1 filter paper and rinsed with 5% HCl and 30 % H2O2 to eliminate the remaining acid until the pH obtained becomes neutral. Then, a strong concrete sheet like mass was recovered on drying under vacuum at 55˚- 60 °C.

CQDs were also synthesized using the green route as described previously with fewer alterations [20]. Briefly, 10 g of pencil peel waste was taken and washed thoroughly with distilled water. Then, pencil peel waste was properly crushed in grinder to get fine powder and the volume was make upto 50 mL with distilled water. For 2 h, the mixture is heated at 80 °C. Then, a brown color solution is obtained on filtering it with Whatman’s No. 1 filter paper. Then, centrifugation was done for 10 min at 4500 rpm and taken by decantation. The obtained solution was then refluxed for 2 h at 80 °C and allowed to cool. Further, centrifuge for 10 min at 4500 rpm and obtained solution was suspended with 1 N 5 mL NaOH [20]. The obtained CQDs solution was observed under UV light for fluorescence and finally stored at 4 °C for future experiments.

The NPs, CQDs and GO synthesized were characterized using different techniques for the confirmation of its composition, size and shape. The techniques utilized were HRTEM, FESEM (MIRA3 TESCAN), EDX and XRD (Bruker D8 Discover).

3D hydrogel utilized in the formation of F− removal pencil was synthesized by sol-gel method. In brief, separate Erlenmeyer flasks were used to take GO and sonication was performed in uniformity for at least an hr in Millipore water (1 mg mL-1) to reach complete dispersion. The obtained diffused suspension of GO was blended with 100 mL of Al2O3 solution (1 N), 100 mL of Ag2O solution (1 N) along with 50 μL of CQDs solution and again sonicate for an hr. NH3 solution of ratio 1:1 was drop-wise added into the suspension after the sonication time is over. It was mixed until the pH of the mixture rises up to ∼7.0. Further, acrylamide (AM) (2 g) was mixed in suspension pursued by the mixing of potassium persulfate which acts as in initiator (7% w/w of AM) and N,N’-methylenebisacrylamide (7% w/w of AM) which acts as a crosslinker through continuous stirring to avoid aggregation in produced hydrogels. Then, jelly like solid mass was kept for 24 h, filtered and rinsed with Millipore water for obtaining neutral pH and desiccated at 60˚- 65 °C in oven. Finally, the obtained hydrogels were crushed and sieved to desirable particle range to carry out the experiments (Fig. 2 ).

The developed prototype (fluoride removal pencil) was developed with 10.5*1.2 cm dimension by combination of nanocomposite GAAC (GO-Al2O3-Ag2O-CQDs), plastic sheet and Whatman’s No. 2 filter paper. In brief, first layer of porous plastic sheet of 75 μm was utilized for with-holding and outer covering, second layer of Whatman’s No. 2 filter paper of 8 μm was used for avoid leaching of nanocomposite in water sample and lastly fine aluminium wire has been attached to the top of pencil for easy dip mechanism. The prototype comprises of 2 gm of adsorbent i.e. GAAC nanocomposite. The developed prototype proves to be an affordable, portable, user friendly and simple in operation for the rural population as well as for the area with limited settling.

F− removal study was conducted with finalized F− concentrations as 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 mg L−1 with 100 mL suspension. The contact time was 2, 4, 6 and 8 min and beakers were placed on a shaker at 120 rpm during adsorption. This adsorption process was influenced by pH variations and was studied in the range of 2–9 and remaining F− content was reported through fluoride ion meter. Removal % was calculated using the given equation.
Removal efficiency (%) = Co − Ce /Co × 100
Where, Co and Ce (mg L−1) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of F-.
Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models were used to calculate adsorption kinetics. To calculate the defluoridation capacity, three chief isotherms were considered as shown in Table1 .
Using the Tukey’s method, statistical analysis was performed at the 5% confidence level using SPSS 15.0.1 on the basis of one factor ANOVA.

Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs, CQDs and GO were synthesized via waste of pencil peel and pencil lead by green and chemical method as explained in experimental section. Morphology and composition of prepared materials was defined through FESEM, HRTEM and EDX spectrum. Fig. 3
(a–f) represents the establishment of Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs and GO. Fig. 4(a–b) shows the formation CQDs.

The FESEM image demonstrates the surface morphology of Al2O3 NPs which was observed as flakes with uneven shape, (Fig. 3a) whereas the surface morphology of Ag2O NPs was spherical (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3e, FESEM image of GO illustrates morphology that it is multilayered in nature with rough surface and wrinkles. The EDX elemental composition found after examination, established that the Al2O3 NPs sample has O (23.34 %), Al (62.85 %) and Si (13.81 %) (Fig. 3b). EDX of Ag2O NPs illustrate the existence of Ag (48.4 %) and O (4.7 %) (Fig. 3d). EDX of GO illustrate the existence of C (86.23 %), O (9.73 %) and Au (4.04 %) (Fig. 3f).

Using high resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HRTEM), CQDs morphology was represented which indicated that the obtained C-dots are circular in shape inconsiderate of the starting material used [21] (Fig. 4a), EDX analysis of CQDs showed the presence of C (94.7 %), O (3.5 %), Na (0.2 %) and Cl (0.3 %) (Fig. 4b).

Before and after F− adsorption, the morphology of surface of GAAC composite hydrogel was studied and shown in Fig. 5 a and c. The analysis of FESEM shows uniform distribution and coating of nanocomposite. The presence of Al, Ag and C in GAAC hydrogel before and after adsorption was confirmed by elemental spectrum (Fig. 5b and d). One extra peak was confirmed by EDX along with respective peaks in GAAC hydrogel i.e. F− adsorption was confirmed by the F− peak from aqueous solution (Fig. 5d).

Calculation of the phase purity and crystallinity of the synthesized Al2O3, Ag2O NPs and CQDs was done using XRD analysis. In synthesized Ag2O NPs, three major diffraction peaks were obtained at 2θ = 31.92˚, 37.98˚ and 54.70˚ [22] (Fig. 6 a) and Al2O3 NPs were 2θ = 32.47˚, 36.92˚, 45.35˚ and 66.17˚ [19] (Fig. 6b). CQDs pattern in XRD shows an intense diffraction peak at 2θ = 30.10˚ [23] (Fig. 6c). However, the crystalline size of NPs was calculated through Debye-Scherrer equation [19].
D =
k λ
β
h k l
cos
θ
h k l
The calculated average crystallite size through Debye-Scherrer equation of Al2O3 NPs was 15.73 nm and Ag2O NPs was 23.38 nm.

The surface functionalization of GO, Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs, CQDs and GAAC before and after F− adsorption was analyzed via FTIR spectra (Fig. 7 ). In GO, CO epoxy stretching, CO alkoxy stretching and CO carbonyl stretching vibrations was attributed by the bands at 1200 cm−1, 1070 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1, respectively; at 3779 cm−1 the absorption band corresponds to the OH stretching [24]. At 574 and 3779 cm−1, characteristic peaks were observed in Al2O3 sample, which are authorized to the metal-oxygen stretching because of the Al-O and OH group, respectively [19]. The adsorption peak at 3781 cm-1, 1596 cm-1 of Ag2O sample correlate to OH stretching and carboxylic group respectively. Meanwhile, at 1042 cm-1 the peak can be described as vibration band of CO whereas band at 2959 and 1484 cm-1 represents bending and stretching of aliphatic C–H respectively. In addition, bands at 860–740 cm-1 are the out of plane vibration bands of NH group and 599 cm-1 corresponds to stretching of metal-oxygen Ag-O [25]. For, CQDs characteristic adsorption peak of − OH stretching could be observed at 3773 and 1095 cm−1and band at 2923 cm−1corresponds to the CH stretching mode. Moreover, the peaks at 1401 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1 and might have occurred due to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively [26]. All the characteristic peaks of GO, Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs and CQDs were also observed in GAAC samples. Ag chelation of NH groups in GAAC was formed due to the increase in intensity of OH and NH band in GAAC spectrum before F− adsorption was indicated. After F− adsorption, the intensity of Al-O and Ag-O band decreased as associated with F− ions interaction and no significant change was observed before or after F− adsorption in GGAC. This indicated that no structural change happen during adsorption process [19]. In addition, decreased intensity of OH bond after F− absorption indicates the substitution of OH ions by F− ions. Similar observations were reported in literature [18,19].

The pH of solution is the very crucial factor for adsorption process. In adsorption process, ionization potential, adsorbate speciation and surface charge of adsorbent are strongly affected by pH of aqueous solution. Since, a release of OH− ions is coupled with anion adsorption, so low pH values favour the F− adsorption over GAAC hydrogel (Fig. 8 a). Fig. 8(a) depict the plot of F− removal % over GAAC hydrogel against the pH solution, which shows that F− elimination % is maximum pH 2. Therefore, the result demonstrated that with the enhancement in pH above 2, F− removal % reduced (Fig. 8a). The development of hydrofluoric acid (HF) is accountable for decreasing of F− adsorption in acidic pH environment. Due to the rivalry for the active surface sites between F− ions and hydroxyl ions, F− remediation decreased under alkaline conditions [27,28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Initial F− concentration and contact time
ce:section.ce:para: For the initial 2 mg L−1 F− concentration, F− removal % was reported as 95.5 %. This concentration further deduced to 80 % for 10 mg L−1 initially F− accumulation at contact period of 8 min (Fig. 8b). May be because of the reduction in amount of accessible adsorption sites because of their saturation at an excessive F− concentration, a difference in the F− removal % is seen. Adsorption performance was calculated as a consequence of contact period from 2 to 8 min with GAAC hydrogel at pH 2 at 30 °C. It is established from the results shown above that with time, adsorption upgrades and an equilibrium state is obtained after a contact period of 8 min (Fig. 8c).

For the calculation of adsorption kinetics, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models were used. The obtained data was utilized for both models to characterize the adsorption kinetics of F− ions on the GAAC. Pseudo-first and second-order model equations [19] are:
log (qe - qt) = log qe - (k1/2.303) t
t/qt = 1/k2qe 2 + t/qe

The parameters estimated in Table 2 shows that pseudo-second-order model is more fit for the adsorption with maximum correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.999) than pseudo-first-order model. Thus, pseudo-second-order shows that the F− adsorption is through chemisorption mechanism.

To calculate the defluoridation capacity, three chief isotherms were considered. The Langmuir isotherm explains the monolayer adsorption and is shown as [19]
Ce/qe = Ce(1/Qo) + 1/Qob

The Freundlich represents heterogeneous surface adsorption and is expressed as [19] (Fig. 9b)
log qe = log kF + (1/n) log Ce
The values of n > 1 stand for the favourable adsorption condition and the obtained n value indicate the favourable isotherm. The attained value of 1/n was 0.426.

The adsorbent-adsorbate interaction is demonstrated by Temkin isotherm, which is defined by the following eq. (Fig. 9c)
qe = RT/bTIn(AT) + RT/bTIn(Ce)
The heat of adsorption values calculated using the Temkin model was 1.51 kJ mol−1.

For F− removal from drinking water, F− removal defluoridation pencil was developed. The developed prototype (Fluoride removal Pencil) with 10.5*1.2 cm dimension was developed by utilizing nanocomposite GAAC (GO-Al2O3-Ag2O-CQDs), plastic sheet and Whatman’s No. 2 filter paper. The developed prototype was made of compact size, portable and cost-effective in respect to maintain its functionality as effective and user-friendly for bottle and glass water defluoridation. We have developed the novel defluoridation prototype for the commercialisation at market scale as most research is focused on the nanocomposites for lab scale defluoridation experiments. To justify the economy of developed defluoridation prototype, we have estimated the cost of market and green route nanomaterials used as silver nitrate market cost: Rs. 4352/25 g vs. green route: Rs. 0.04; Al2O3 market cost: Rs. 171/500 g vs. green route: Rs. 0.14; NaOH market cost: Rs. 270/500 g vs. green route: Rs. 0.001. The estimated cost of the developed one defluoridation prototype is about US ̴ $ 0.023. The low cost of developed defluoridation prototype proves that it is more approachable and affordable for the rural population as well as for the areas having limited resources (Fig. 10 ).

The parameters of water sample collected have been pre and post tested with the help of different techniques and instruments. Electrical pH meter and conductivity meter CDM210 were used to measure the pH and electrical conductivity of the water sample. Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometer was employ to measure chemical constituents of the sample in the chemistry department at Banasthali Vidyapith, Rajasthan (India). The concentration of nitric acid (HNO3 −) and carbonate (CO3 2−) were analyzed via titrimetric method; chloride (Cl−) and sulfate (SO4 2−) were measured via ion chromatography in the laboratory of the Banasthali Vidyapith. Ion-selective electrode was used to study total F- concentration in the water samples [29].

The F− removal efficiency for drinking water sample with 10 mg L-1 by using defluoridation pencil was calculated. In Table 4 , shows the outcome that the defluoridation pencil had a strong affinity for F− removal in the presence of other anions (Cl-, HCO3-, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, NO2-, NO3-). Developed hydrogel (GAAC) exhibited removal percentage for 2 and 10 mg L-1 F- as 95.5 and 80 %, respectively. The removal % of F− decreased from about 95.5%–80% at ambient temperature. The fall in % removal may occur because some anions rivalry and others enhanced columbic repulsion forces for the active sites with F− [30]. After the adsorption study, the concentrations of F− were quiet below the permissible value defined by WHO (above 1.5 mg L-1). Subsequently, the novel defluoridation pencil is an effective and promising and efficient material F− elimination from water sample along with other harmful anions (Cl−, HCO3-, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, NO2-, NO3-) and also reduces the other parameters such as total dissolved solid, conductivity, pH of the sample to be well improved level for the drinking purpose.
Each of the F− adsorption experiments was conducted thrice and all the data depicts the average values of triplicate experiments.

The alumina is a lewis acid so it forms a nanocomposite with graphene oxide. The formed nanocomposite with Al2O3 also has hydroxyl groups bonded with Al atom and can undergo nucleophilic substitution with F− ion. The carboxylic group of graphene oxide get bonded with aluminium ions. When this nanocomposite is treated with CQDs in presence of Ag2O, a tetrahedral complex was formed. The CQDs and aluminium nanocomposite get reduced by Ag2O. This complex has hydroxyl groups which can be displaced by F- ion present in the water under treatment (Fig. 11 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of F− adsorption efficiency for various adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison of GAAC hydrogel adsorption efficiency with other reported adsorbents (Table 5 ). GAAC hydrogel in the study has higher adsorbent capacity than other adsorbents in the literature.

The defluoridation pencil was prospered comprising of GAAC nanocomposite for F− removal. 3D Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs, CQDs, GO based (GAAC) hydrogel was prepared by sol-gel process for the removal of F−. In the hydrogel, 2D GO sheets and Al2O3 NPs, Ag2O NPs, CQDs chains were cross-linked, forming a 3D network porous and amorphous structure. Adsorption of F− was conducted at different pH range 2–9, initial concentration was 2–10 mg L-1 and contact time 2, 4, 6 and 8 min. On the basis of Langmuir isotherm model, the maximum adsorption capacity of F− can reach 12.04 mg g−1. The removal % for 2 and 10 mg L-1 F− concentration was 95.5 and 80 % at pH 2 and 8 min. The result of adsorption isotherm showed that the Langmuir model (R2 = 0.978) was fitted better than the Freundlich model (R2 = 0.928) with F− adsorption. Therefore, constitute the ability for the application of monolayer coverage of F− onto the surface of GAAC hydrogel. Pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.999) was favoured divulged that the insight of F− ions took place through chemisorption processes on GAAC hydrogel.

Neha Singh carried out the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Sonu Kumari analyzed the data and prepared the graphs. Suphiya Khan designed the project and organized the research. All authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by DBT BIG-BIRAC Ref No. BIRAC/CCAMP0723/BIG-13/18 and DST major project entitled “Low Cost-Renewable Energy Driven (LC-RED) Water Treatment Solution Centre” Ref No. DST/TM/WTI/WIC/2K17/124. We acknowledged bioinformatics centre for providing computational facilities.
